evals.name: hello-world
evals.expected_output: Creates a widget with valid structure (widget.json + dist/content.html), HTML fragment with heading and paragraph, runs registry build.

assertions.name: creates-widget-json
assertions.text: Creates a widget.json file with required fields (version, title, description, category, source)

assertions.name: creates-content-html
assertions.text: Creates a dist/content.html file that is an HTML fragment (no <html>, <head>, <body> tags)

assertions.name: mentions-registry-build
assertions.text: Mentions or instructs running ./bin/build-registry.sh to rebuild/validate the registry

evals.name: analytics-tracking
evals.prompt: Add analytics tracking to our community
evals.expected_output: Identifies this as a global script (not a widget). Asks about which analytics provider and which pages to target. Creates script.json with appropriate page targeting rules.

assertions.name: identifies-script-type
assertions.text: Identifies this as a global script rather than a widget

assertions.name: asks-about-targeting
assertions.text: Asks which pages the script should load on, or which analytics provider to use

assertions.name: describes-script-structure
assertions.text: Describes or plans the correct directory structure: scripts/<name>/ with script.json and script.js

evals.name: brand-colors
evals.prompt: Make the community match our brand colors
evals.expected_output: Identifies this as a global stylesheet. Asks about brand colors, typography, and which elements to target. Creates stylesheet.json with rules.

assertions.name: identifies-stylesheet-type
assertions.text: Identifies this as a global stylesheet rather than a widget

assertions.name: asks-about-brand
assertions.text: Asks about specific brand colors, typography, or which elements to style

assertions.name: describes-stylesheet-structure
assertions.text: Describes or plans the correct directory structure: stylesheets/<name>/ with stylesheet.json and style.css

evals.name: polished-card
evals.prompt: Build a widget that shows a user profile card with avatar, name, and role
evals.expected_output: Creates a polished profile card widget with consistent spacing, typography hierarchy, subtle shadows/borders, and hover/focus states on interactive elements.

assertions.name: consistent-spacing
assertions.text: CSS uses a consistent spacing approach — values follow a recognizable scale (multiples of 4px/8px, or rem units) rather than arbitrary pixel values like 13px, 37px, etc.

assertions.name: typography-hierarchy
assertions.text: Output has at least 2 distinct heading/text levels with different font-size and/or font-weight, creating clear visual hierarchy (e.g., name is larger/bolder than role)

assertions.name: visual-polish
assertions.text: Card uses at least 2 of: border-radius, box-shadow, subtle border, background color differentiation, or gradient — not just plain unstyled divs

assertions.name: avatar-styling
assertions.text: Avatar element has explicit styling — border-radius (circle or rounded), defined dimensions, and object-fit or background-size for image handling

assertions.name: creates-valid-structure
assertions.text: Creates widget.json with required fields and dist/content.html as an HTML fragment

evals.name: data-table
evals.prompt: Build a widget that displays a table of customer health scores with columns for customer name, score, and trend
evals.expected_output: Creates a data table widget with clear visual structure: distinct header row, row separation, right-aligned numbers, and loading/empty states.

assertions.name: header-distinct
assertions.text: Table header row is visually distinct from data rows — uses different background color, font-weight, or border styling

assertions.name: row-separation
assertions.text: Table rows have clear visual separation — zebra striping (alternating row colors), borders between rows, or spacing/gap between rows

assertions.name: number-alignment
assertions.text: Numeric columns (score) are right-aligned or use tabular-nums/monospace for number display

assertions.name: loading-or-empty-state
assertions.text: Widget handles at least one non-happy-path state: loading indicator, empty state message, or error state — not just the populated table

assertions.name: creates-valid-structure
assertions.text: Creates widget.json with required fields and dist/content.html as an HTML fragment

evals.name: linear-hero
evals.prompt: Build a hero section widget inspired by linear.app — dark theme with clean typography
evals.expected_output: Fetches linear.app for design reference, extracts specific design tokens (dark background, clean sans-serif, gradient accents), and creates a widget that reflects those design decisions.

assertions.name: fetches-reference
assertions.text: Uses WebFetch to retrieve linear.app for design inspiration

assertions.name: dark-theme-applied
assertions.text: Output uses a dark color scheme (dark background with light text) matching the reference site's aesthetic

assertions.name: clean-typography
assertions.text: Uses a system or sans-serif font stack with deliberate font-size/weight/letter-spacing choices — not browser defaults

assertions.name: gradient-or-accent
assertions.text: Incorporates at least one gradient, glow effect, or distinctive accent color inspired by the reference design

assertions.name: creates-valid-structure
assertions.text: Creates widget.json with required fields and dist/content.html as an HTML fragment

evals.name: vague-request-multiturn
evals.prompt: Build me a widget to promote my latest product release
evals.expected_output: Turn 1: Proposes direction, asks focused questions. Turn 2: User says 'banner, match stripe.com' — agent must fetch the reference and extract design cues. Turn 3: User says 'go ahead'. Agent builds with design inspired by reference, configurable content, SDK compliance.
turns: A banner would work. Make it match our site — stripe.com is the reference for the look and feel.

assertions.name: turn1-focused-questions
assertions.text: In turn 1, asks at most 3-4 clarifying questions — does not dump 5+ questions at once

assertions.name: turn1-proposes-approach
assertions.text: In turn 1, proposes a specific visual direction (e.g., 'banner with headline and CTA') before receiving the user's answer

assertions.name: turn1-no-premature-build
assertions.text: In turn 1, does NOT create any extension files — waits for user answer before building

assertions.name: turn2-fetches-reference
assertions.text: In turn 2, agent uses WebFetch to retrieve stripe.com and extracts design cues (colors, typography, layout patterns)

assertions.name: turn2-proposes-design
assertions.text: In turn 2, agent proposes a concrete design based on the reference — mentions specific colors, fonts, or layout choices extracted from stripe.com

assertions.name: design-reflects-reference
assertions.text: Final CSS reflects design choices informed by stripe.com — not generic defaults (e.g., uses colors, typography, or layout patterns that can be traced to the reference)

assertions.name: configurable-properties
assertions.text: Final widget.json has configuration properties for content that the admin can edit (headline, CTA text, or similar)

assertions.name: html-fragment
assertions.text: Final dist/content.html is an HTML fragment (no <!DOCTYPE>, <html>, <head>, or <body> tags)

assertions.name: sdk-compliant
assertions.text: Final widget code uses sdk.$() for DOM queries and sdk.getProps() or template variables for configuration

assertions.name: creates-valid-structure
assertions.text: Final output includes widget.json with required fields (version, title, description, category, source) and dist/content.html

evals.name: nps-score-multiturn
evals.prompt: I need something that shows our NPS score on the community homepage
evals.expected_output: Turn 1: Identifies connector need, asks about data source and visualization. Turn 2: User says 'Delighted, API key' — gives no endpoint, no visualization preference. Agent must fill gaps: propose visualization, figure out connector details. Turn 3: User says 'go ahead'. Agent builds.
turns: We use Delighted. It needs an API key I think.

assertions.name: turn1-identifies-connector
assertions.text: In turn 1, identifies that displaying NPS data requires a connector to fetch from an external API

assertions.name: turn1-asks-data-source
assertions.text: In turn 1, asks which service or API provides the NPS data before building

assertions.name: turn1-no-premature-build
assertions.text: In turn 1, does NOT create any extension files — waits for user answer before building

assertions.name: turn2-fills-gaps
assertions.text: In turn 2, agent proactively addresses what the user didn't specify — proposes a visualization style AND connector design (endpoint URL, auth configuration) rather than asking the user to look it up

assertions.name: creates-apikey-connector
assertions.text: Final connectors.json uses auth_type 'apikey' and get_secret() — no hardcoded secrets

assertions.name: uses-sdk-connector-execute
assertions.text: Final widget code calls sdk.connectors.execute() to fetch data from the connector

assertions.name: reasonable-visualization
assertions.text: Final widget displays the NPS score with intentional visual treatment (color coding, gauge, large number, or trend indicator) — not just raw text dump

assertions.name: html-fragment
assertions.text: Final dist/content.html is an HTML fragment (no <!DOCTYPE>, <html>, <head>, or <body> tags)

assertions.name: creates-valid-structure
assertions.text: Final output includes widget.json with required fields and dist/content.html

evals.name: layout-hint-small
evals.prompt: Build a widget that shows our current app version, keep it really small and unobtrusive
evals.expected_output: Turn 1: Proposes a compact form factor (badge, pill, bar) and asks about data source. Turn 2: User confirms and says 'just a config property'. Agent builds a compact, minimal widget — not a full card or section.
turns: Yeah a pill or badge sounds right. Just make it a configurable text property, go ahead and build it.

assertions.name: turn1-proposes-compact-layout
assertions.text: In turn 1, agent proposes a compact form factor — badge, pill, tag, bar, or inline element — rather than a card, section, or full-width component

assertions.name: turn1-no-premature-build
assertions.text: In turn 1, does NOT create any extension files — waits for user answer before building

assertions.name: compact-layout
assertions.text: Final widget uses a compact form factor with tight dimensions (small max-width or max-height, minimal padding) rather than a full card or section layout

assertions.name: minimal-footprint
assertions.text: Final CSS constrains the widget size — uses small fixed dimensions, display: inline-block/inline-flex, fit-content, or tight max-width (under ~200px) to keep it unobtrusive

assertions.name: restrained-styling
assertions.text: Final visual styling is understated — no large shadows, heavy borders, or prominent backgrounds; uses subtle treatments appropriate for a small utility element

assertions.name: creates-valid-structure
assertions.text: Creates widget.json with required fields and dist/content.html as an HTML fragment

evals.name: layout-hint-dashboard
evals.prompt: I need to see all our key metrics at a glance
evals.expected_output: Turn 1: Proposes a multi-column grid/dashboard layout and asks about which metrics and data source. Turn 2: User specifies metrics and says 'configurable, go ahead'. Agent builds a dashboard-style widget with grid layout.
turns: Active users, NPS score, open tickets, and avg response time. Just make them configurable number properties, build it.

assertions.name: turn1-proposes-grid-layout
assertions.text: In turn 1, agent proposes a multi-column, grid, or dashboard-style layout for displaying multiple metrics — not a single stacked list

assertions.name: turn1-no-premature-build
assertions.text: In turn 1, does NOT create any extension files — waits for user answer before building

assertions.name: grid-or-multicolumn
assertions.text: Final layout uses CSS grid, multi-column flexbox (flex-wrap), or explicit column structure to display metrics side-by-side rather than a single vertical stack

assertions.name: multiple-metrics
assertions.text: Final widget shows 4 distinct metrics (active users, NPS score, open tickets, avg response time)

assertions.name: metric-visual-treatment
assertions.text: Each metric has intentional visual treatment — large prominent number with smaller label, color coding, icon, or trend indicator

assertions.name: scannable-hierarchy
assertions.text: Layout is designed for quick scanning — metric values are visually dominant (larger font-size or bolder weight) over labels and secondary info

assertions.name: creates-valid-structure
assertions.text: Creates widget.json with required fields and dist/content.html as an HTML fragment

evals.name: github-integration
evals.prompt: Build a widget showing our latest releases from github.com/acme/product
evals.expected_output: Turn 1: Identifies GitHub API connector need, proposes token/apikey auth, asks about display preferences. Turn 2: User gives minimal confirmation. Agent fills gaps — correct GitHub API endpoint, auth config, visualization. Turn 3: User says 'go ahead'. Agent builds.
turns: Yeah that works. We have a token for it.

assertions.name: turn1-identifies-connector
assertions.text: In turn 1, identifies that fetching GitHub releases requires a connector to the GitHub API

assertions.name: turn1-proposes-github-api
assertions.text: In turn 1, references the GitHub API (api.github.com) or GitHub REST API as the data source — not scraping the HTML page

assertions.name: turn1-no-premature-build
assertions.text: In turn 1, does NOT create any extension files — waits for user answer before building

assertions.name: turn2-fills-gaps
assertions.text: In turn 2, agent proactively proposes the GitHub releases endpoint, auth configuration, and visualization — doesn't ask the user to look up API details

assertions.name: correct-api-endpoint
assertions.text: Final connectors.json uses a GitHub API releases endpoint pattern (e.g., api.github.com/repos/acme/product/releases)

assertions.name: creates-token-connector
assertions.text: Final connectors.json uses auth type 'apikey' with get_secret() for the GitHub token — no hardcoded secrets

assertions.name: uses-sdk-connector-execute
assertions.text: Final widget code calls sdk.connectors.execute() to fetch data from the connector

assertions.name: shows-release-info
assertions.text: Final widget displays release information (name, date, or version) — not just raw JSON

assertions.name: html-fragment
assertions.text: Final dist/content.html is an HTML fragment (no <!DOCTYPE>, <html>, <head>, or <body> tags)

assertions.name: creates-valid-structure
assertions.text: Final output includes widget.json with required fields and dist/content.html

evals.name: known-service-auth
evals.prompt: Build a widget that pulls data from our Stripe dashboard
evals.expected_output: Turn 1: Identifies Stripe API connector need, proposes apikey auth, asks what data to display. Turn 2: User gives minimal confirmation. Agent fills gaps — correct Stripe endpoint, auth config, visualization. Turn 3: User says 'build it'. Agent builds.
turns: Recent payments. We have an API key for it.

assertions.name: turn1-identifies-stripe-connector
assertions.text: In turn 1, identifies that pulling Stripe data requires a connector to the Stripe API

assertions.name: turn1-proposes-auth-pattern
assertions.text: In turn 1, proposes or mentions API key authentication for Stripe — does not ask the user to figure out the auth type

assertions.name: turn1-no-premature-build
assertions.text: In turn 1, does NOT create any extension files — waits for user answer before building

assertions.name: never-asks-for-key-value
assertions.text: Agent never asks the user to paste, provide, or share the actual API key value — uses get_secret() pattern instead

assertions.name: turn2-fills-gaps
assertions.text: In turn 2, agent proactively proposes Stripe API endpoint and visualization style rather than asking the user to specify them

assertions.name: creates-apikey-connector
assertions.text: Final connectors.json uses auth type 'apikey' with get_secret() — no hardcoded secrets

assertions.name: correct-stripe-endpoint
assertions.text: Final connectors.json uses a Stripe API endpoint (api.stripe.com) for fetching payment or charge data

assertions.name: uses-sdk-connector-execute
assertions.text: Final widget code calls sdk.connectors.execute() to fetch data from the connector

assertions.name: html-fragment
assertions.text: Final dist/content.html is an HTML fragment (no <!DOCTYPE>, <html>, <head>, or <body> tags)

assertions.name: creates-valid-structure
assertions.text: Final output includes widget.json with required fields and dist/content.html
